results.title: ToolSense Alternative: Offline + AU/NZ | MapTrack
results.content:
## TL;DR - ToolSense vs MapTrack

## What is ToolSense?

ToolSense is a Vienna-based Asset Operations Platform (repositioning to FM Operations per CEO Alexander Manafi's April 2026 statement) covering QR-code asset tracking, IoT (GPS, runtime, battery, fuel sensors), work orders, maintenance, inspections, scheduling and lifecycle management. Founded in 2017 by Alexander Manafi, Benjamin Petterle and Rostyslav Yavorskyi. Closed a Series A round of EUR 8M in December 2022 (PwC IIoT lead); a FM-Operations repositioning round led by GRO Capital was announced 13 April 2026. [...] | AU data residency | YesAWS Sydney with AU data residency; AS/NZS-aware templates | Not publicly confirmedAWS + GCP multi-region; no documented AU residency commitment(per ToolSense security, Jun 2026) |
| AI asset creation | YesAI-assisted asset creation from photo (live) | Not publicly confirmedAI Assistant described as "upcoming" on its own page(per ToolSense AI Assistant, Jun 2026) |
| GPS & telematics integrations | Yes150+ telematics, fleet & fuel-card data sources (vendor-neutral) | ?Cleaning OEMs (Karcher, Hako, TASKI, PUDU, Nexaro, CenoBots); construction via AEMP 2.0 standard(per ToolSense product, Jun 2026) | [...] ToolSense is Vienna-based (founded 2017) and is repositioning to FM Operations per CEO Alexander Manafi's statement around its 13 April 2026 GRO Capital round. Its Capterra vendor reply dated 12 August 2024 said offline mode would 'go live in a couple months', but offline is still not listed as a feature as of June 2026. ToolSense states 200+ customers; the 500,000+ machines figure is attributed to its customer ISS, not to ToolSense's own scale. Further AU/NZ expansion may follow but is not currently active.

Spotted something out of date or incorrect? Email corrections@maptrack.com and we will review and update this page.

# GRO backs ToolSense to scale AI-powered Asset Operations Platform

### Copenhagen, Denmark – April 13, 2026 – GRO today announces an investment in ToolSense, an Austria-based company building the leading AI-powered facility management (FM) Operations Platform. The investment marks GRO’s first in Austria, further expanding its presence in the DACH region. [...] ToolSense helps FM providers, dealers, and equipment manufacturers run smarter operations by connecting assets, workflows, and real-time data in one platform. Combining SaaS, IoT, AI, and deep robotics integrations, the platform replaces paper, Excel, and WhatsApp with automated, data-driven operations - reducing downtime, cutting costs, and improving performance across the entire FM value chain.

results.title: 10 Best Tool Tracking Softwares for 2026
results.content:
#### Key Features

#### Tracking Technology

ToolSense connects to equipment via IoT sensors, OEM cloud integrations, and QR codes for non-connected assets. The IoT layer differentiates it from QR-only competitors. The platform processes 250,000 IoT messages per day across 50,000 connected assets globally.

#### Pricing and ROI

Custom pricing tied to deployment size and connected asset count. ToolSense reports a 20% reduction in machine investment costs and up to 80% reduction in downtime for facility management customers. ISS Austria cut machine investment costs by 20% by extending equipment lifecycles using ToolSense data. [...] ### Choosing Your Tool Tracking Solution

The tool tracking software market splits into three categories. Purpose-built tool tracking solutions like GoCodes Asset Tracking, TOOLTRIBE, ShareMyToolbox, ToolHound, GigaTrak, and ToolWatch deliver the deepest functionality for trades and construction teams. General asset platforms like Asset Panda, Sortly, and EZOfficeInventory offer broad flexibility but require you to source your own hardware. Facility management platforms like ToolSense extend asset tracking into broader operations through IoT integration. # ToolSense Framework Audits LLM Tool Retrieval for Agent Reliability

James Whitfield - AI Herald Author Avatar

James Whitfield

LLM tool retrieval   parametric tool knowledge   AI agent reliability   ToolSense framework   LLM fine-tuning   enterprise AI agents

   ToolSense Framework Audits LLM Tool Retrieval for Agent Reliability  

New diagnostic framework ToolSense reveals 15-25% accuracy drops in LLM parametric tool retrieval. Essential for developers building reliable AI agent

  

## New Diagnostic Tool Exposes Critical Weaknesses in LLM Tool Retrieval [...] Researchers have released ToolSense, a diagnostic framework designed to audit how large language models (LLMs) recall tool knowledge after parametric training, revealing that even top-performing models suffer from systematic retrieval failures when faced with semantically overlapping or rare tool descriptions. According to a paper published on arXiv (2606.12451), the framework measures both memorization accuracy and retrieval robustness in LLMs fine-tuned as tool retrievers, a growing necessity as enterprises deploy LLM agents over catalogs containing thousands of specialized APIs.

## Why Developers Should Care About Parametric Tool Retrieval [...] ## Implications for Enterprise AI Deployments

For organizations building LLM agents over internal tool catalogs—such as CRM tools, cloud service APIs, or financial data queries—ToolSense provides a much-needed quality assurance layer. Rather than blindly trusting parametric retrieval, developers can now run automated audits before deployment. The framework also helps identify tools requiring additional training data or description rewriting.

results.title: ToolSense: A Diagnostic Framework for Auditing Parametric Tool ...
results.content:
## Limitations [...] We introduce ToolSense, an open-source LLM-powered diagnostic framework that takes any
tool catalog as input and automatically generates three diagnostic benchmarks:
(1) a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries in three ambiguity tiers reflecting how human users
actually write queries;
(2) an MCQ probing benchmark testing discriminative factual knowledge about tool
capabilities; and
(3) a QA probing benchmark testing inferential factual knowledge about tool properties.
Alongside the benchmarks, we establish a free-form evaluation protocol that reports an
Internalization Score (IS@k = free@k / constrained@k) as a trie-dependency diagnostic. [...] We introduced ToolSense, a diagnostic framework that auto-generates three benchmarks—RRB, MCQ, and QA—from any tool catalog to probe parametric retrieval systems along orthogonal axes: OOD generalization, trie-dependency, and factual knowledge retention.
Applying ToolSense to ToolBench reveals that high constrained recall on verbose in-distribution queries is a poor proxy for real-world capability: performance collapses on realistic queries, hierarchical tokens exhibit deep trie-dependency, and Stage 2 retrieval fine-tuning systematically erodes the tool knowledge built during Stage 1, leaving models that behave more like lookup tables than knowledge bases.
